title: The Politics of a Family Recipe
sentences: Every family, it seems, guards at least one recipe with disproportionate seriousness.
Ours was a modest stew, yet arguments over its preparation were anything but modest.
Each relative insisted their version was the original, despite scant evidence either way.
I once assumed such disputes were trivial, little more than nostalgic stubbornness.
Over time, though, I began to see the recipe as a stand-in for something less tangible.
Preserving it exactly, imperfections included, felt like a way of preserving memory itself.
When I finally cooked it for my own children, I deviated from it almost without noticing.
That small betrayal, oddly, felt less like loss than like the recipe quietly evolving.
Perhaps authenticity was never about precision but about who gathers around the table.
The stew, in the end, mattered rather less than the ritual surrounding it.
